summary:
The Battle of Yorktown (September–October 1781 AD) was the decisive final engagement of the [[event:106|American Revolutionary War]], at which the combined American and French forces under George Washington and Rochambeau besieged and captured the British army under General Cornwallis, effectively ending major military operations and forcing Britain to negotiate peace. The Yorktown campaign was a masterpiece of coordination between American and French land and naval forces: Washington's army, together with Rochambeau's French troops, marched rapidly from New York to Virginia (a 500-mile movement the British in New York failed to detect in time), while the French fleet under De Grasse sailed north from the Caribbean and won the decisive naval Battle of the Chesapeake (September 5, 1781) against the British fleet — blocking British naval relief of Cornwallis.

Cornwallis found himself trapped on the Yorktown peninsula with approximately 8,000 men, besieged by an Allied force of 19,000. The formal siege proceeded with textbook efficiency: parallel trenches were dug approaching the British lines, redoubts were stormed in night assaults, and Allied artillery systematically demolished the British works. On October 19, 1781, Cornwallis surrendered — claiming illness, he sent a subordinate, General O'Hara, to present his sword. O'Hara attempted to surrender to the French Rochambeau rather than the American Washington (a slight Washington refused, requiring the surrender to his own American deputy, Lincoln). The 8,000 British troops marched out to the tune 'The World Turned Upside Down.' Yorktown did not immediately end the war (peace negotiations took two more years), but it effectively shattered British political will to continue fighting, leading to the [[event:2477|Treaty of Paris]] (1783) recognizing American independence.
